title: A Nested Factor Model for Equity Markets: Reconciling Multifractal Stock Returns and Rough Index Volatilities
abstract: arXiv:2505.02678v4 Announce Type: replace  Abstract: The Nested factor model was introduced by Chicheportiche et al. to represent non-linear correlations between stocks. Stock returns are explained by a standard factor model, but the (log)-volatilities of factors and residuals are themselves decomposed into factor modes, with a common dominant volatility mode affecting both market and sector factors but also residuals. Here, we consider the case of a single factor where the only dominant log-volatility mode is rough, with a Hurst exponent $H \simeq 0.11$ and the log-volatility residuals are ''super-rough'' or ''multifractal'', with $H \simeq 0$. We demonstrate that such a construction naturally accounts for the somewhat surprising stylized fact reported by Wu et al. , where it has been observed that the Hurst exponents of stock indexes are large compared to those of individual stocks. We propose a statistical procedure to estimate the Hurst factor exponent from the stock returns dynami

Title: A Nested Factor Model for Equity Markets: Reconciling Multifractal Stock Returns and Rough Index Volatilities
Abstract: arXiv:2505.02678v4 Announce Type: replace  Abstract: The Nested factor model was introduced by Chicheportiche et al. to represent non-linear correlations between stocks. translated_title: 股票市场的嵌套因子模型：调和多分形股票收益与粗糙指数波动率
translated_abstract: 嵌套因子模型由Chicheportiche等人提出，用于表示股票之间的非线性相关性。股票收益由标准因子模型解释，但因子和残差的（对数）波动率本身被分解为因子模式，其中有一个共同的占主导地位的波动率模式影响市场因子、行业因子以及残差。本文考虑单因子情况，其中唯一的占主导地位的对数波动率模式是粗糙的，Hurst指数H≈0.11，而对数波动率残差是“超粗糙”或“多分形”的，H≈0。我们证明，这种构造自然解释了Wu等人报告的一个令人惊讶的风格化事实：即股票指数的Hurst指数相对于个股的Hurst指数更大。我们提出了一种统计方法，从股票收益动态中估计Hurst因子指数。
tldr: 本文通过嵌套因子模型，用粗糙的主波动率模式和超粗糙的多分形残差，成功解释了股票指数Hurst指数大于个股的反直觉现象。
en_tdlr: This paper uses a nested factor model with a rough dominant volatility mode and super-rough multifractal residuals to successfully explain the counterintuitive phenomenon that stock index Hurst exponents are larger than those of individual stocks.
